summary: Jeff Atwood announces his departure from his dream job at Vertigo Software to pursue opportunities created by his blog's success. Using the Choose Your Own Adventure books as an extended metaphor, he reflects on the risk of leaving a secure position for entrepreneurial unknowns. He commits to building things rather than becoming a 'full-time blogger,' citing respected industry figures who both write and create. The post hints at an upcoming startup venture with an unnamed collaborator, which would later become Stack Overflow.
key_points: Choose Your Own Adventure books were early programmer training, teaching branching logic and decision trees through interactive storytelling
Leaving a secure job for entrepreneurial risk is scary, but 'if it's not a little scary, then it's not the right choice'
The most respected people in tech aren't just writers—they build things too
Full-time blogging without building is a 'cop-out' that produces vapid commentary
Like the books' outcomes, most career paths have more failure states than success states, but you still have to choose
money_quotes: I feel like I'm still playing Choose Your Own Adventure.
Sometimes choosing your own adventure means closing one door to open another.
I'm not sure what exactly lies on page 10.
It's scary to trade the security of a safe, salaried job for the unknowns of your own small business.
If it's not a little scary, then it's not the right choice.
I refuse to become a full-time blogger. I think that's a cop-out.
They're not just excellent writers and communicators. They build stuff, too.
The world has enough vapid commentary blogs. I want to build stuff—and talk about it.
key_insight: The most meaningful career moves require accepting risk and choosing to build things, not just comment on them.
